name: Graph Retrieval Study
tagline: Compare retrieval strategies for research note grounding.
objective: Find which retrieval setup gives the best planning and explanation quality for weekly research work.
summary: This project tests how note chunks, paper highlights, and code summaries should be combined so Research Brain can answer from real project state instead of generic chatbot priors.

canonical.name: Graph Retrieval Study
canonical.description: Compare how notes, papers, and code summaries should be retrieved so Research Brain can reason from real project state.
goals: Measure planning quality across different retrieval mixes
Prefer grounded next-step recommendations over generic responses

linkedPapers.title: Grounded Planning for Research Agents
linkedPapers.relevance: Supports separating durable project memory from short-horizon planning context.

evaluationCriteria: Suggested next steps must reference project-specific evidence
Planning responses should identify the right source mix

attempted: Reviewed whether mixed-source retrieval improves planning prompts
changed: Added examples showing where note-only retrieval missed implementation context
failed: Simple lexical matching did not rank code summaries strongly enough
learned: Planning prompts need both notes and code summaries when project details are sparse

attempted: Answered user question: What should I do next?
changed: Recommended: Run a five-prompt evaluation comparing note-only retrieval against mixed-source retrieval for planning questions.
Proposed next step: Run a five-prompt evaluation comparing note-only retrieval against mixed-source retrieval for planning questions.
failed: Referenced blocker: Planning rubric is still informal
learned: Grounded response used sources: Planning prompt review, Retriever prototype summary, Grounded Planning for Research Agents

attempted: Answered user question: Why is that the next step?
changed: Recommended: Run a five-prompt evaluation comparing note-only retrieval against mixed-source retrieval for planning questions.
Proposed next step: Run a five-prompt evaluation comparing note-only retrieval against mixed-source retrieval for planning questions.
failed: Referenced blocker: Planning rubric is still informal
learned: Grounded response used sources: Grounded Planning for Research Agents, Grounded Planning for Research Agents, Planning prompt review

action.recommendedNextStep: Run a five-prompt evaluation comparing note-only retrieval against mixed-source retrieval for planning questions.
successCriteria: Each prompt has outputs from both retrieval settings
A clear winner is identified for grounded planning quality

contextSources.title: Weekly retrieval log
contextSources.summary: Mixed-source retrieval helps with planning questions when note coverage is sparse.

contextSources.title: Grounded Planning for Research Agents
contextSources.summary: Suggests separating durable memory from short-horizon planning context.

contextSources.title: Retriever prototype summary
contextSources.summary: Current prototype ranks chunks with a simple lexical match and needs source-aware scoring.

name: Literature Synthesis Workflow
tagline: Turn reading notes into project decisions instead of disconnected summaries.
objective: Design a repeatable workflow for linking paper insights to active experiments and code changes.
summary: This workspace tracks how papers should influence project direction, metrics, baselines, and concrete implementation decisions inside Research Brain.

canonical.name: Literature Synthesis Workflow
canonical.description: Turn paper reading into project consequences such as experiments, metrics, and implementation changes.
goals: Create a paper review template that names project impact
Link literature insights to code and experiment decisions

linkedPapers.title: Scientific Workflow Memory Systems
linkedPapers.relevance: Shows why durable project memory matters across research sessions.

evaluationCriteria: Each paper review names a concrete downstream action
Reviews should surface mismatches with the active project setup

attempted: Reviewed why prior paper notes did not alter roadmap decisions
changed: Defined a need for paper summaries to point to experiments or code changes
failed: A summary-only format did not create actionable project updates
learned: Paper notes are only useful when attached to a current project decision

action.recommendedNextStep: Draft one project-aware paper review template and test it on two recent papers.
successCriteria: The template captures method, assumptions, and project consequences
Two papers can be processed without adding extra fields

contextSources.title: Paper linking notes
contextSources.summary: Most reading workflows fail because they do not name what should be built or measured next.

contextSources.title: Scientific Workflow Memory Systems
contextSources.summary: Shows that stable project memory improves continuity across research sessions.

name: Repo Handoff Operator
tagline: Decide when a research question should become a Codex execution task.
objective: Make Research Brain route implementation-shaped requests into concrete Codex handoffs without over-escalating.
summary: This project defines the boundary between chat reasoning and execution so the system can stay grounded in current repository state and only hand off work that actually needs code changes.

canonical.name: Repo Handoff Operator
canonical.description: Route user requests between planning/explanation and repository execution using current project state.
goals: Avoid sending vague questions to Codex
Escalate code-changing requests when repository action is clearly required

linkedPapers.title: Execution-Aware Agent Routing
linkedPapers.relevance: Supports separating reasoning tasks from tool-execution tasks.

evaluationCriteria: Router should classify planning vs implementation requests correctly
Handoffs should include a clear reason grounded in project state

attempted: Tested simple keyword routing for implementation-shaped prompts
failed: The router could not explain ambiguity when prompts mixed planning and code work
learned: Routing needs a reason field so the user can understand why Codex is or is not selected

blockers: No ambiguity handling for mixed planning and implementation prompts
action.recommendedNextStep: Add a small set of routing examples that cover explanation-only, planning, and code-execution requests.
successCriteria: Each routing example has an expected worker and explanation
At least one mixed-intent prompt is handled explicitly

contextSources.title: Router design notes
contextSources.summary: Routing should stay conservative and only hand off to Codex when code changes or execution are clearly needed.

contextSources.title: Router heuristic summary
contextSources.summary: The current router uses keyword matching and does not yet consider blockers or project state.
